summary: When my family was in Burma, life was very hard. We grew food on our farm, but it was never enough to feed everyone. During the war, Burmese soldiers killed many Karen people and destroyed homes and farms because they wanted our land. We had to run away to survive, selling the food we had grown to get money before escaping.

After escaping from Burma, my family moved to the Mae La Oon camp. Life there was easier because we didn't have to work as hard planting food every morning. We received free food monthly through notes given by camp workers, but if we wanted more, we had to work to earn money. The amount of food we got depended on how many people were in our family.

About sixteen years after living in Mae La Oon camp, my family heard that Americans were helping refugees apply to go to the United States. The interviews and health checks for our family took place in Mae Sot. It was there that we started the process to move to America, which changed our lives for the better.

When my family finally moved to the United States of America, we began a new life full of hope. America gave us freedom, good food, and free education, which made life much easier than in Thailand. We are very thankful to God for blessing us with the chance to live in America, where we no longer have to fear Burmese soldiers or struggle for basic needs.

After fleeing Burma, my family lived in Thailand, specifically in refugee camps like Mae La Oon. Life in Thailand was difficult because we depended on limited food rations and had to work to get extra food. We also lived with the constant fear of Burmese soldiers, so moving to America felt like a true blessing compared to our life in Thailand.
